data.title: Golub Capital's Dividends Are At Risk
data.description:
I've owned Golub Capital's (GBDC) shares for quite some time and really enjoyed the value creation GBDC offered to its shareholders for the last couple of years. When I look at the long-term perspective, I see that GBDC has managed to uphold a comparable NAV (slightly increased) while creating over $20 per share of additional value to its shareholders through dividend payments (regardless of the valuation itself).

The last time I covered GBDC, I assigned it a "hold" rating due to increasing risk around its portfolio with a changing macroeconomic environment. Since then, its stock price has decreased by 6%. As a result, I see GBDC trading at an even deeper discount than it used to, with a P/BV ratio of 0.92x.

As GBDC has just shared its quarterly report, I decided to take another look, and I can say right from the get-go - its dividend is at risk, and I see a high probability for a dividend cut. Let's go through all that step by step.

GBDC's Portfolio Performs Worse With Decreasing Interest Rates, and We Can See That Through Several Metrics

Do you remember the booming BDC space as interest rates were increasing since Q3 2022? I remember that very well, as that was the time I established many of my BDC positions in the first place. Still, the party seems to be over - just look at what started to happen since the second half 2024. GBDC's investment income yield has decreased from 12.8% in fiscal Q2 2024 to 10.4% in fiscal Q4 2025. That drop, combined with no meaningful impact on the side of the cost of debt, was substantial enough to result in a much lower weighted average spread on GBDC's investments. How much did GBDC's spreads decline? According to the chart below, we're talking about a 220 bps lower spread, which is nearly linear to income yield changes (240 bps).

What does this chart tell me? The key insight I derived from it is that the theoretically lower cost of debt won't be nearly enough to offset the negative influence that decreasing rates have on GBDC's yields.

Are you surprised? I'm not, especially given GBDC's asset mix - let's keep in mind that despite GBDC's involvement in equity stakes (7% of the portfolio), the portfolio is still oriented around debt investments (93%). Out of these debt investments, 99% are structured with a floating rate, making GBDC highly susceptible to interest rate changes.

As a result, I and all of GBDC's shareholders had to watch its net investment income decline from $0.47 in fiscal Q4 2024 (on an adjusted basis) to $0.39 as of the last quarter. That's a major decline amounting to ~17% year-over-year, which is not a thing I'd like to ignore.

Before I get to the hard talk I've prepared for you regarding GBDC's ability to uphold dividend payments, I'd like to mention something else - how about a positive note?

When I reviewed GBDC's presentation, I was very happy to see that its portfolio companies have a top-tier credit quality represented not only by a non-accruals ratio amounting to 0.3% (at fair value) but also by stable performance ratings. If you review the table below, you'll notice the same thing I noticed - GBDC doesn't have 1-rated investments, and the share of 2-rated investments is low (~1%). At the same time, 4- or 5-rated investments look very well and amount to nearly 89.5%. Such a healthy performance structure indicates solid performance of GBDC's portfolio and limited risk of defaults.

Let's Not Sugarcoat It - GBDC May Be Forced to Cut Its Dividend

I don't like to sugarcoat anything, so I'll state this as clearly as I can - there's a high risk that GBDC will cut its dividend. Unfavorable interest rate changes have a strong impact on its portfolio's income potential, and as I mentioned earlier, a lower cost of debt will not save the situation. GBDC's NII is much lower than it used to be, and there's nothing suggesting a turnaround in that trend.

Yes, there's a chance that a more active transactional market will provide GBDC with a better deal flow and more opportunities to choose from, but still - just take a look at the relation between its adj. NII and regular dividend payments. Based on the most recent financial data, I see that GBDC reached just 100% coverage, which essentially means that one more cut or any random default could be enough to drive its regular dividend coverage below 100%.

If that tendency continues for a longer period of time, GBDC will be forced to make changes in its distribution policy, as it won't be able to support it anymore. And I believe the management has proved that they are taking a closer look at GBDC's dividend policy. Don't get me wrong - the management hasn't made any decisions (at least so far), but my prediction is that we will see GBDC cut its regular dividend in 2026. To quote directly from the Earnings Call:

Before I pass the mic to Tim and Chris to go over operating results in more detail, I want to comment on the decision by our Board to declare a $0.39 per share distribution for the first fiscal quarter of 2026. In connection with this decision, the Board also determined that it would be prudent to revisit GBDC's dividend policy early next year when we hope to have more information on the forward outlook for rates and asset spreads and financing costs. GBDC plans to approach the dividend question with the same underlying strategy we've had since our IPO.

To remind those of you who haven't heard me talk about dividend strategy before, we're guided by four goals. First, we seek to maintain a stable net asset value per share over time. Second, we seek to minimize excise taxes over time. Third, we seek to adjust our base distribution level infrequently. And finally, we seek to pay as high a dividend yield on NAV as sustainable, consistent with the above goals, the three prior goals. Now we can't always achieve all four of these goals at the same time. And at such times, we need to find the right balance.

My Conclusion is Simple - I'm Still Not Buying GBDC, but It Don't Think It's Time to Sell Either

While some investors consider discounted BDCs as great opportunities, I'm more careful on this front. Please keep in mind that a discounted valuation is a double-edged sword - yes, you can secure an attractive entry price, but that given BDC is also less effective when gathering capital. Why is that? The higher the valuation premium, the more capital you can collect by issuing equity.

On top of that, GBDC's portfolio is hurt by the most recent interest rate dynamic - I verified that based on its investment yields, investment spreads, and NII. Nothing suggests a turnaround on that front, so I think it's reasonable to assume that GBDC's dividend coverage isn't safe anymore. Quite the opposite - given the management's communication and just 100% coverage of regular dividends, I expect GBDC to cut its quarterly distributions in 2026.

On the positive side, I don't consider GBDC overvalued due to the discount it has to its NAV, and I think it has one of the best portfolios in the field from the credit quality perspective. The risk of defaults is well-mitigated, and, assuming diligent review of internal performance ratings, GBDC's portfolio companies are quite healthy.

With all that taken into account, I'm going to hold onto my shares and continue collecting dividends (even if they turn out to be lower than initially expected). Therefore, GBDC is a "hold" for me. Cheers!

data.title: Credit Card And BNPL Lenders Paint A Brighter-Than-Expected Picture Of The Economy
data.description:
Introduction:

With a number of economic factors pointing to a weakening economy, such as a multitude of negative jobs revisions and record low consumer sentiment, it is no wonder why investors may be worried about the state of the consumer.

When analyzing the state of the consumer, investors must understand that the consumer is exposed to many different types of loans in their daily life, including, but not limited to:

  • Credit Cards & BNPL (Buy Now, Pay Later)
  • Mortgages
  • Auto Loans
  • Student Loans

Although there are many types of loans available, it is usually the unsecured credit that tends to lead in delinquencies. As such, I would like to dive deeper into credit card and BNPL loan health metrics, and analyze what some of the biggest players within the industry are projecting for the future. This will help investors understand the magnitude of the consumer weakness that is currently plaguing the US economy.

My Stance

With that in mind, I strongly believe that the consumer may not be as weak as what some information out there may say. One of these points is the nearly record-low consumer sentiment:

This metric, from the University of Michigan has recently shown one of the lowest readings to date, at only 51.0, which is closest to the all time low of 50.0, which occurred during the pandemic. But is the economy really in such a dire state?

The analysis below demonstrates that the key players within one of the most crucial segments of the US economy, unsecured credit via credit cards, are not showing nearly the same signs of stress.

Despite the fact that some firms over the last 2 years maintained elevated balances to deal with losses, data shows that some of these reserves were revealed to not be depicting the reality of the current consumer health landscape.

With this in mind, my stance on the overall economy is that although recent data points to a weakening consumer, unsecured credit trends actually show neutral to favorable metrics, signaling that consumer-related economic problems are over-exaggerated in today's markets.

Key Players

In formulating this analysis, I would like to look into only 3 of the largest credit card issuers, in order of purchase volume, are:

  • JP Morgan Chase & Co. (JPM)
  • American Express Company (AXP)
  • Capital One Financial Corporation (COF)

These issuers were chosen as they represent the middle-class, upper class and lower class consumers, respectively, with a large enough sample size on each to be able to extrapolate to the entire economy as a whole.

Additionally, as one of the leading US players in BNPL, I would like to analyze Klarna Group plc (KLAR). Since the purchase volume is still quite minimal within this space relative to the overall unsecured credit market, BNPL does not significantly change the overall consumer outlook.

When analyzing these companies for weakness of the consumer, the key metric to look into is each company's reserve for credit losses. This contra-asset account is the firm's anticipation for future losses.

More specifically, this analysis will plot the following metrics, to better understand how far or below each firm is to their historical averages.

  • Net-Write Offs
  • Delinquencies (30+ Days Past Due)
  • Reserves as a % of Card Member Loans

Net-Write Offs and Delinquencies will be compared to their pre-pandemic levels, to better understand the current consumer's position in comparison to the pre-recession numbers.

It is important to mention that delinquencies usually turn into Net Write-Offs, and it can be expected that when delinquencies increase, Write-Offs will follow in the next quarter.

Meanwhile, Reserves as a % of Card Member Loans cannot be analyzed on a pre-pandemic comparison basis. Starting January 1st, 2020, many banks, including the 3 mentioned below, adopted the U.S. Financial Accounting Standards Board's CECL (Current Expected Credit Loss) Standard, which pushed many banks to significantly increase their reserves to comply with the standard. The standard forced banks to be more forward looking with their provisions for credit losses, instead of reactive, by reacting to net-write offs occurring within the quarter.

Thus, with this standard in place, it is difficult to fully quantify pre-pandemic reserve levels in comparison to today's levels. However, it is still possible to look for trends within each firm's reserves to better understand where we may end up in a few quarters from now.

Consumer Health, By Company

JP Morgan Chase

With over $235 billion total card member loans outstanding, this makes JP Morgan Chase the largest US consumer credit issuer.

Unlike the later mentioned American Express and Capital One, which represent the edges of the economic classes in the US, JP Morgan takes a broad portfolio approach. The bank offers both higher end products that often compete with American Express's luxury niche, as well as more mass-market offerings, often with no annual fees, that compete with Capital One. This broad approach, along with being the US's largest credit card issuer, JP Morgan Chase is the most vital data point in this analysis.

Looking at JP Morgan Chase's Net-Write Offs and Delinquency rates for the past 6 years, both of these metrics saw a strong increase from their 2021-2022 levels, as consumers who were increasingly worried about inflation and job security, decided to cut back from discretionary spending, and save extra to fund their living expenses. This conscious choice, along with stimulus from the government, allowed consumers to address some of their loan balances, which included credit card balances.

Since that point in time, JP Morgan Chase has consistently showed heightened levels of Net Write-Offs, above the ~3% rate for Net-Write Offs, and ~1.8% rate for delinquencies seen pre-pandemic.

These above-average numbers could reflect consumer difficulty in fighting the elevated inflation levels, affecting prices of everyday items and services. In the words of CNBC, consumers "may have overextended themselves".

Although consumers maintained prudence during the early pandemic times, inflation remained stubbornly high. With consumers stepping foot outside of their homes after quarantines began to end in 2022, increased consumer activity eventually slowed down, as individuals began to feel the cumulative effects of inflation and money-printing during the pandemic.

Looking at JP Morgan Chase's reserves for credit losses, the picture looks a lot more stable. While there has been a significant increase in Net Write-Offs since the bottom near late 2021-early 2022, reserves for credit losses are currently relatively flat compared to this time period, where reserves represented about 6-6.7% of JP Morgan's total outstanding card member loans. Despite the elevated Net Write-Offs seen currently, the latest reading remains around the same, at 6.6%. This signals that JP Morgan currently does not anticipate a near term consumer credit collapse.

My above statements are further reinforced by some statements from the firm's latest Q3'25 earnings:

While we are closely watching the potentially softening labor market, our credit metrics, including early-stage delinquencies remain stable and slightly better than expected.

Therefore, I believe that investors can surmise a relatively favorable image of the overall consumer credit landscape from this data.

American Express

American Express is a unique company to analyze, representing the premium credit card segment. Consumers within this space tend to be the most resilient, and least prone to carrying a balance or defaulting on their loans.

Not surprisingly, this has led to lowest-in-industry metrics for Net Write-Off and Delinquency Rates. As per the latest Q3'25 earnings call, management mentioned:

Credit performance remains very strong and stable. Q3 delinquency and write-off rates were low with delinquency rates flat to last quarter, while write-off rates declined. This performance is supported by our focus on premium products, which tend to attract high-income, highly creditworthy customers. - Christophe Le Caillec, CFO of American Express

Keeping in mind this demographic, it is important to remember that while American Express accounts for a large portion of the US credit card consumer base, it is disproportionally weighted towards creditworthy and resilient consumers. Data taken from the financial tables of Amex, from the latest period to Q1'19, shows the following trends:

As shown above, both Net-Write Offs and Delinquency Rates are below their pre-pandemic levels. These metrics saw a strong increase from their 2021-2022 levels, as consumers started to feel the effects of heightened inflation.

Since those times, both Net-Write Offs and Delinquency Rates have normalized to more appropriate levels, representing a healthier economic state where consumers feel less pressure to save.

From the two metrics above, American Express is not seeing any significant stress within the consumer space, and the same can be said when analyzing reserves for credit losses.

The above chart depicts a moderation of American Express' reserve balance, which in the last 2 years has stayed steady around 4%. Although this is higher than pre-pandemic levels, which stayed around 2.7%, the rates are not alarming, and means that Amex is not currently projecting any above-average losses in its future. In fact, American Express actually released a portion of its reserves in Q1'25.

All of these data points suggest that the upper-class consumer is currently not feeling the negative effects of the economy, and continues paying off a majority of its debts on time and in full.

Capital One

Capital One, being the smallest of the three banks, when analyzed by purchase volume, is still another vital data piece to understand the true state of the US consumer.

Despite being smaller, Capital One's credit card services represent the lower economic class consumer. This is highly evident in the firm's well-above average net write off and delinquency rates.

Since pursuing this cohort is a conscientious choice from Capital One, there is no need to focus on the absolute values. Rather, I would like to focus on the trends observed, both before and after the pandemic.

As seen above, the Net Write-Off and Delinquency Rates are relatively similar compared to the pre-pandemic levels, though they have recently been elevated significantly.

I believe that these elevated Net Write-Offs and Delinquencies are simply a more amplified version of the data we saw from JP Morgan Chase. Capital One clients, being in the lower brackets of the economy, are the most cash-strapped. This means that they are most prone to bearing the effects of the immense inflation the consumer has had to face in recent years, and the cumulative effect of this is resulting in consumers feeling some pressure. Regardless, CEO Richard Fairbank mentions in the latest earnings call that this recent improvement in Net Write-Offs was significantly larger than expected:

The third quarter is the seasonal low point for our card losses. But the linked quarter improvement we saw was significantly beyond what we would expect from normal seasonality.

Our charge-off rate has been improving on a seasonally adjusted basis throughout 2025, following the trend of improving delinquencies that started in late 2024 and supported by strong recoveries. - Richard Fairbank, CEO & President

Looking at the reserves for credit losses, Capital One creates an even brighter narrative.

Although reserves were elevated since 2023, Capital One has recently released a significant amount of reserves, as they see "continued favorable credit performance". From their recent Q3'25 presentation, statement highlighted in orange:

This recent release of allowance for credit losses, signals that Capital One does not anticipate any significant increase in write-offs in the near future. This is incredibly favorable to see from a bank that deals with the consumer that is most prone to defaults, and is usually the first to do so in the overall economic landscape.

Klarna

Straying away from credit cards, I would like to look into the US's leading BNPL provider, Klarna. Despite being part of a secular trend, it is important to note that Klarna's GMV is only $32.7 billion, a steep decline from any of the major banks mentioned above. Therefore, it does not have a large effect on the overall market, but is still worth discussing.

Klarna recently made headlines by significantly increasing provisions for credit losses. At the surface level, this indicates that the firm may see a decrease in the confidence behind the resilience of the consumer.

However, taking a look past the headline figure shows that the firm's realized losses remained relatively flat over the last year, ranging between 0.39%-0.48%.

While it is true that provisions for credit losses increased significantly (+28 bps Y/Y), this increase, as mentioned in the slide above is tied to Klarna's "Fair Financing" service, which is a service designed to allow consumers to finance larger ticket items over a long duration, often with an interest rate. Klarna has recently committed to substantially expand this service, as it gives the firm more ability to penetrate the US market, and take share away from competition.

Management further reaffirmed the health of the consumer in the Q3'25 earnings call with the following statement:

In fact, when you look at fair financing, you can see delinquencies falling 5% year-over-year, while GMV has been growing at 139%. Similarly, U.S. charge-offs remain stable within expected ranges.

Therefore, since the actual realized losses from Klarna remain low, I believe that the BNPL segments reveals to investors a cautious but optimistic outlook, which is similar to the relatively in-line outlook that was observed from the much larger credit card market.

Risks

Although consumer credit has shown optimistic signs, as with any macro outlook, some risks to the overall consumer landscape still exist. Today, those risks are mainly centered around the recent employment numbers.

Multiple jobs reports in the past months revealed negative jobs growth. For now, no numbers show a cratering of the jobs market, but simply a slow decline that is causing the unemployment rate to tick up.

The most recent employment report, posted on November 20th, revealed a beat, with a sudden jump in employment numbers. However, the unemployment rate continued its steady tick-up, now to 4.4%. In addition to this, despite the positive numbers, multiple negative revisions have been made to previous months, and it can be expected that this month may get revised down as well.

This problem is further exacerbated by the recent government shutdown, which, created a major hole in the economic data for investors, something the Fed had referred to as "driving in the fog".

This, coupled with still high inflation creates a difficult landscape for the Fed - who has to choose between saving the labor market or taming inflation.

It is not fully clear how the employment situation will play out. However, the one thing that is clear, which my analysis hopes to highlight, is that the consumer is still relatively healthy and is not struggling to pay off their balances as may be implied by the general market.

Outcome

Overall, since pre-pandemic times, it is no doubt that we have seen a gradual weakening of the consumer. The consumer, especially within the lower brackets of the economy, was first hit by high unemployment, driven by COVID-related shutdowns.

This shutdown of the economy prompted the government to print billions of dollars in stimulus checks, leading to inflation levels that, unfortunately, have not moderated to levels seen before the pandemic. These factors, combined with a recent weakening of the job market, all signal to one of the weakest consumer periods in recent history.

However, as revealed by unsecured credit lenders, such as JP Morgan Chase, American Express, Capital One, and even Klarna, the picture is clear. The consumer is definitely not in a time of record strength, as was seen immediately after the pandemic. However, the consumer is also significantly stronger than what some metrics may project, such as the University of Michigan consumer sentiment rating. Nearly all above data from credit lenders showcases that the consumer is either meeting or exceeding pre-pandemic credit health metrics.

Below is a tabulated summary of all information derived from this analysis.

Company Delinquencies and Net Write-Offs (vs Pre-Pandemic) Reserves for Credit Losses Highlights
JPM In-Line Stable "Delinquencies remain stable and slightly better than expected"
AXP Decreased Stable Reserves release in Q1'25
COF In-Line Rapidly Improving Reserves release in Q3'25
KLAR Decreased Increased, but situation remains steady Increased reserves for credit losses tied to "Fair Financing" deal, rather than consumer health

Although all these players operate within slightly different segments, the overall message is evident, which is that the consumer is currently more resilient than the market may project. The major firms recognize this, and have been releasing their reserves for credit losses, signaling that even those who work with consumer credit the closest do not anticipate a major decline in strength in the near future.

Thus, despite certain risks, these recent trends push me to have a positive take on the overall consumer landscape and the macro environment as a whole.

data.title: Consolidated Edison: A Tricky Balancing Act
data.description:
In the fall of 2022, I believed that shares of Consolidated Edison (ED) were electrified. The company was selling its renewable business in order to fund massive investments needed in its asset base, as relative valuations looked rich, with utility businesses being tricky to value in my point in my view.

Despite the boom in demand for electricity, driven by electrification of the economy as well as the rise of demand from datacenters and AI, shares of Consolidated Edison have seen total returns of just about 35%, far trailing the market at large. This and the fact that the company hit the political headlines is enough of a reason to update the thesis here.

A Diversified Utility

Consolidated Edison Company of New York is a major utility provider for the city and immediate vicinity. The company delivers electricity to about 3.7 million customers, gas to over a million customers and steam to about 1,500 clients. Besides the activities in the city, the company caters some clients with its Orange & Rockland Utilities business as well, while it has a Transmission business as well.

The company faces quite some challenges because of its unique geography, high building intensity as well as complex infrastructure, which creates a real hurdle on top of the general hurdles seen in the energy transition and electrified future as well.

When I looked at the shares in 2022, the company has reported 2021 sales at $13.7 billion, largely generated from electricity sales, accompanied by natural gas and steam, transmission and clean energy revenues. Operating profits of $2.8 billion left net earnings around $1.2 billion, equal to earnings of $3.85 per share.

However, the company was already in full investment mode, with capital spending seen at $4 billion seen, twice the depreciation charge. Traded at $85 per share, equity valuation came in at $30 billion, as net debt of $25 billion yielded a $55 billion enterprise valuation. Debt was increasing substantially because of these capital expenditure needs, in combination with dividends, which were payable at $3.10 per share each year.

Addressing the natural net debt built up, the company sold its Clean Energy business to RWE Renewable America in a $6.8 billion deal, designed to combat rising net debt, and badly needed to fund a $15 billion capital spending program in the years 2022/2023/2024, as well as to pay for a juicy near 4% dividend yield.

However, I was leaning cautious, as utility companies face a tough operating environment, with some consumers and clients having decentralized production (although this is much less the case in NY), creating a tougher job for the assets of a utility business, certainly as the company itself was divesting its renewable assets.

Growing, But At A price

Forwarding to early this year, the company reported annual revenues of $15.3 billion, up 4% on the year before, with core electric sales up by nearly 7% to $11.6 billion. Operating profits were reported at $2.7 billion as adjusted earnings came in at $5.40 per share, having made real inroads since 2022.

Net debt came in close to $25 billion again, with the proceeds from the Clean Energy business having gone to fund net capital expenditures. While revenue growth has been seen in a meaningful way, reported sales growth has been limited (accounting for the divestment).

In March of this year, the company sold 6.3 million shares in order to shore up its balance sheet amidst continued investments made into the business. This followed a fairly similar-sized equity raise in the fall of 2024 already.

Real growth has been seen so far this year, with reported revenues up around 11% in the first nine months of the year. This comes amidst rising prices and rising volumes, although that the company has been diluting its share price at a pace of 4% per annum here.

While nominal sales and earnings growth has been delivered upon, net debt jumped to $25 billion here, with capital spending totaling nearly $4-$5 billion per annum here. Year to date, adjusted earnings are up 40 cents to $4.82 per share, on track to come in close to $6 per share this year.

Now trading at $100 per share, resulting earnings multiples look reasonable at 16–17 times, as the dividend yield has come down to 3.4% as the dividend has been hiked at a relatively more modest pace in recent years, making that the dividend yield has been creeping lower, as the payout ratio has been moving down a bit, also in part designed to improve free cash flow generation here.

And Now?

With some 361 million shares trading at $100, the market value of the company stands at $36 billion, for an enterprise valuation in excess of $61 billion. Given these trends, the outlook is more of the same. Consolidated Edison sees stronger demand, but this comes as leverage is quite high already, with continued dilution now seen in the shareholder base, with dividend growth lagging earnings growth.

All this makes me still rather cautious, similar to 2022. The company is benefiting from stronger demand, but this benefit comes with rapid growth in capital expenditures, which results in dilution and higher debt, as near term revenue impact is relatively modest. Moreover, improvements in earnings are very limited in relation to the multi-billion spent into the business.

Given these long dynamic conditions ahead, Consolidated Edison recently has become somewhat of a political play. This came after president Trump and mayor-elect Mamdani urged for price cuts, opposed to a proposed 10-11% increase in electric bills and natural gas prices.

The question is how things will evolve under the democratic leadership, following the outcome of the election of mayor Mamdani, with high rates becoming a real political debate here. In fact, nearly half a million customers are enrolled in affordability programs here.

However, I see no quick resolution to this, if only for the huge capital spending programs planned, seen at a cumulative $72 billion in the decade 2025-2034, which really provides no room to reduce prices, as the most credible roadmap is for prices to surpass inflation.

What Now?

While Consolidated Edison looks relatively cheap here and strong results look good on paper, I fear the political scrutiny and the huge capital expenditures to be incurred in order to grow.

This is evident in a diluting shareholder base, making it very hard to built conviction here, just like was the case in 2022, as growth really comes with a double-sword here.

data.title: Aduro's Pilot Is Finally Here, Yet I'm Staying On Hold Until Data Arrives
data.description:
It’s been a few months since I first analyzed Aduro Clean Technologies (ADUR), and it has moved forward in the intervening period. My July article on the company recommended a Hold on the stock because I was waiting for some tangible progress with its execution, and we have that now. The company’s pilot plant is now a physical asset that is in commissioning, and it has a stronger balance sheet after the June capital raise. Alongside those, it is also reaping the rewards of having a COO who has actual scale-up experience. All very nice.

At the same time, the company is still pre-revenue and burning cash, so we don’t yet have undeniable commercial proof that what it offers is truly as valuable as we expect. Yet, the share price has moved ahead of the fundamentals and the proof I’d like to see from the pilot. So, I’m recommending another Hold position.

First, though, here’s a brief reminder of what the company is about. Aduro is a chemical technology company based in Canada that is using proprietary platforms like its HCT (Hydrochemolytic Technology) to convert low-value feedstock (like mixed-waste plastics and heavy crude oils) into higher-value chemicals and fuels, among other things.

Now, I’ll get into what its latest results tell us. These are for Q1 2026, although I may discuss some of the details from its FY25 results.

It’s Making Good Progress On Execution

When I was reading the reports from the previous two quarters, the main thing I latched on to was the fact that Aduro has finished building its pilot plant. That plant has been a big talking point over the last year or so, and given that all the mechanical, automation, feedstock system, and safety work is done, I’m far more willing to stick my neck out on its prospects.

We have a commercialization path and timeline, and that sequence is something you’d only really see when the pilot is close to running. The company spent over C$1.5 million in capex in Q1, so I’m not surprised that operating costs were much higher too.

I also like the way the new COO, David Weizenbach, is influencing the company’s framing of its commissioning and process readiness. His experience and expertise are evident in the structure around automation, hazard analysis, and QA in the September corporate presentation, where it is much stronger than it was a year ago. That also leads me to its commercial roadmap, where we can see how Aduro plans to go from running a 10-kg-per-hour pilot to a one-ton-per-hour demonstration unit and eventual commercial deployments.

To be clear, I’m not saying that this guarantees anything, but it definitely shows us that the company now has a logical path to follow from data gathering to real projects, and the milestones are clearer.

Then, we have industrial engagement, and again, its corporate presentation from September tells us that there is real interest in what Aduro has to offer. For one, it is working with NexGen to “explore the development of a demonstration-scale HCT plant,” per its FY25 results. It is also working with entities like Cleanfarms to test whether it is feasible to use agricultural plastic waste like bale wrap, grain bags, silage film, and others as feedstock for its HCT platform. I cannot overstate how impressive this is, because these are real-world, dirty, mixed olefins that would destroy pyrolysis economics. If they fit in HCT’s wheelhouse, then we’ll have some more evidence that Aduro is pushing its process towards the hardest problems in plastics recycling.

It has also tested synthetic turf plastics as feedstock for its HCT. I’d call the results encouraging because the company says that several entities are interested in them, so there’s a lot of demand for recycling solutions in this area.

However, I think the TotalEnergies CEP collaboration is still the most crucial validation partnership for Aduro. It’s been on since October 2023, and it is still in the evaluation phase. That tells me that the larger strategic players still see HCT as a candidate technology, and Aduro needs to prove that the pilot economics work if it wants to convert that interest into revenue streams.

Why I’m Still At A Hold

It’s really simple: the gap between Aduro’s technical progress and its financial reality is why I’m holding for now.

Q1 2026’s numbers make this obvious. The company made around C$44,500 in revenue, all from evaluation services. I’d label what it calls commercial activities as small CEP engagements, and they’re useful for validation, but they don’t matter in terms of its operating performance.

Conversely, let us look at the company’s cost structure. Opex is up because of the commissioning and commercialization. R&D and G&A spending doubled and then some on a year-over-year basis, and share-based compensation (SBC) was over C$2 million in the quarter. On a cash-only basis, Aduro spent well over C$4.5 million in Q1.

Remember, this is a company that had ~C$15 million in cash at the end of the quarter, so there are around 4 quarters of runway left if it continues spending at this rate. So, we’re likely going to see another capital raise soon as it reaches the design and engineering phase of the one-ton-per-hour demonstration plant.

I must also touch on dilution. Between the Nasdaq offering, the warrant liabilities, higher share-based incentives, and the higher expenditure profile one expects of a company in Aduro’s position, it’s obvious that its capital structure is going to grow pretty quickly. I understand why, but I’m pointing it out because the current valuation already prices the company as if major dilution won’t affect the long-term returns negatively and in a big way.

I’d say the stock is trading mostly based on hype and expectation, and I’m not surprised. What we consider to be transformative technology or innovation definitely justifies some of that bullishness, but there isn’t any pilot data to justify it yet. I believe that the market is assuming that Aduro will have a smooth commissioning process, strong yield and product quality, manageable pre-processing requirements, and a clean path to demonstration scale, and none of that has been proven yet.

Finally, we still don’t know what the commercial economics look like. Ideally, we would first see continuous-run data, material balances, and some evidence that the reaction pathways across multiple feedstocks are stable. Then, it will be easier to determine whether HCT is a viable, scalable industrial process.

What Turns This Into A Buy For Me

The first thing I am looking for is clean commissioning and stable early runs. That means the mechanical side of its reactor system holds up, the Siemens automation stack behaves the way it should in a continuous chemical process, and the unit can run at steady-state conditions without constant intervention. In my opinion, Aduro achieving that means that the company moves from development to demonstration readiness. As an aside, I should also mention that the company recorded a fairly major win with steam-cracking its plastic-derived Hydrochemolytic Oil a few days ago, so it’s definitely doing some things right.

The second piece is data. I want to see real output distributions, yields, and conversion quality across multiple feedstock types. The technology is built around the flexible handling of polyolefins, mixed waste streams, and even plastics, so the early material balances will matter. The main questions are simple:

  • What fraction of the feed converts to usable hydrocarbons?

  • How clean is the product stream after separation?

  • How much pre-processing is actually required to keep the process stable?

The third thing I’m watching is partner behavior because interest is one thing, but commitment is another. For me, any indication that TotalEnergies or another major CE or petrochem player is willing to advance beyond the evaluation stage would be even more meaningful.

Finally, I’m looking for signs that the financial profile is settling. I know that the pilot capex will fade, but operating expenses need to stabilize quickly. Obviously, the burn does not have to drop quickly, but I want to see that the company can operate without accelerating costs each quarter.

data.title: Lockheed Martin: Profitability Is A Problem
data.description:
Lockheed Martin (LMT) has long been viewed as a defensive stalwart and an anchor holding steady through economic cycles while benefiting from a stable stream of government contracts. The company's scale, entrenched positioning in U.S. defense spending, and legacy programs give it an aura of reliability that many investors instinctively gravitate toward. LMT has a market cap of $106.6 billion and offers a 2.86% dividend yield that has grown for 22 years.

As a global aerospace company, LMT researches, designs, and develops technology systems, products, and services for Aeronautics, Missiles, Fire Control, Rotary, Mission Systems, and Space. The stock is up a meager 20% over 5 years, down 12.5% over one year, while being 11% lower since early October.

I rate LMT a sell. Their recent Q3 earnings showed some growth, but a wider view shows the company has struggled to grow in recent years, especially compared to competitors. Their margins continue to fall, as the balance sheet also weakens. They are valued fair compared to the sector, but reality shows the stock trades at its largest premium in years.

Earnings & Growth

Q3 reported adjusted earnings of $6.95, handily beating expectations. Revenue was $18.61 billion, up 8.8% YoY. Management slightly raised their 2025 revenue guidance, which is the one piece to the puzzle that has grown over time, albeit very inconsistently, as seen above.

There has not been any earnings growth for an extended period of time for LMT. For example, the five Q3 EPS results from 2021 to 2025 are all between $6.77 and $6.95. Revenue has gained, but net income is about 35% lower than its peak in 2020. Lack of execution has really hurt LMT, with a $1.6 billion pretax loss related to an Aeronautics program and multiple helicopter programs, which was outlined in Q2 earnings.

Inefficiencies & Falling Profit

Return on invested capital (ROIC) is half of where it was a few years ago, relating to the faltering new program losses. The drop in ROIC can be connected to the significant fall in EBIT, which is $5.92 TTM, off from over $9.0 billion in 2023, again due to the inefficient program additions.

Current assets are up just over 25% since 2021, while current liabilities are almost 65% greater in that time. Accounts payable increases and unearned revenue are driving that change, which now has their quick ratio at 0.93.

Total debt for LMT is $22.2 billion, almost double what it was in 2021. Expenditures have risen big time, and so far, results have not been where management hoped in terms of generating greater profit.

In fact, profitability is arguably the biggest concern with LMT. Gross margin is at its lowest mark in over a decade at 8.25% and about 4x lower than the sector median. Net income margin has also been smacked down from 9.4% to 5.7% YoY. It's all related, and as already mentioned, the unexpected programs are a driver, but so is the majority of their fixed-price contracts. Any supply chain, labor, or inflationary cost increases are just absorbed by LMT, and the company hovers around 60% of contracts being fixed.

Valuation

LMT has a PE ratio of 25.77, just above their sector average. I think a fairer valuation based on the stock's history is much closer to 18x. This is the stock's highest valuation since October of 2022, when it traded at 28x. In the year following, LMT stock dropped 16%. The current valuation is not justified as earnings have not grown. I have LMT as trading at a premium.

Peers

Among a few historical and newer players in the aerospace industry, LMT has been at the bottom of the leaderboard on performance this year. They are very slightly trading at more of a discount, but it is not validated with their historical valuation and spot in line behind the group in growth and profits. Boeing (BA) has seen its inconsistencies in profits, but the other three take the cake easily. From a past and forward-looking growth perspective, LMT is at the bottom of the barrel. While each defense stock differs in their capabilities slightly, the industry as a whole seems to be in a growing environment based on global conflict and countries ramping up safety productions. Based on momentum, expected growth, and profitability, there are other stocks in the industry I would prefer to invest in.

Risks to Thesis

LMT has continued to see an uptick in their backlog amidst their growing pains. This does show they have the capabilities to keep expanding their revenue. Although sales have not been the issue, as they have been steadily on the rise. It's the behind-the-scenes poor execution on deployed capital that is the problem.

I do believe the defense sector will perform well over the next several years, and I wouldn't be surprised if LMT outgains segments of the broader market. But that is not a super safe bet in my eyes, and I see other similar stocks as a better investment.

Conclusion

LMT has been a leader in defense for decades, but the time has come when the company is being caught up with by others in the industry amidst struggles of their own. I need to see some earnings growth for multiple quarters and improvement in operations on their capital investments. I will be seeking management's guidance as to the direction they head on improving efficiencies with upcoming service or product releases to keep pace with competitors. I see the stock as trading at a premium and can't stomach how large the drop has been in bottom-line profits. Sell LMT and give another look in mid-2026.

data.title: RIV: Fund Of Funds With An Appealing Distribution Yield
data.description:
The RiverNorth Opportunities Fund (RIV) is a closed-end fund designed to enhance distribution income for investors by investing in closed-end funds, exchange-traded funds, special purpose acquisition companies [SPACs], and business development companies [BDCs]. The strategy has a forward distribution rate of $1.53/share, yielding 13.38%.

About RiverNorth Opportunities Fund

RIV was launched on December 24, 2015, by RiverNorth Capital Management as a multi-asset strategy designed to optimize income for investors. The strategy takes a diversified approach to allocating assets by selecting managed strategies and SPACs to drive growth and income, allocating across equities, fixed income, and BDCs. RIV has a management fee of 177 bps and a number of other fees for a total expense ratio of 669 bps.

The fund has weighting caps to be used as guardrails for investment rotation:

  • Fixed Income, Funds, & SPACs: 60%
  • Global Equities: 30%
  • Emerging Market Equities: 15%
  • Real Estate: 10%
  • Energy Master Limited Partnerships [MLPs]: 15%

Within fixed income, no more than 30% of the total assets can be allocated to high-yield or senior loans. CEFs held in the strategy are selected using quantitative analysis, primarily to select funds that are trading at a discount to the net asset value [NAV] with the expectation of the discount narrowing. The fund may also utilize total return swaps to enhance returns in the portfolio with a weight cap of 15%.

According to RIV’s most recent annual report, CEFs accounted for 59.64% of the total portfolio weight. The top holding within the asset class was Pershing Square Holdings at 12.04%. Pershing Square Holdings largely invests in large-cap companies, with its top holdings including Alphabet (GOOG), Amazon (AMZN), and Brookfield Corporation (BN). Interestingly enough, RIV had a short position in BN per its last annual report.

SPACs were the second largest allocation in RIV, making up 40.38% of the total portfolio weight. The largest holding within the SPACs category was the PIMCO Dynamic Income Strategy Fund (PDX) [per the annual report], though it is an income-oriented global credit markets fund. The largest SPAC holding was the Ares Acquisition Corporation II at 1.8%. At the end of September 2025, the company de-SPACed as Kodiak AI (KDK), an AI-powered autonomous vehicle technology company. KDK has since traded down from its initial trading price with an enterprise value of $1.22b, well below its $2.5b valuation prior to going public.

Since its annual report, RIV has reduced its exposure to SPACs from roughly 40% down to 28%, likely to derisk the portfolio while focusing on other assets like BDCs, ETFs, and debt.

On October 8, 2025, RIV announced the issuance of 5.9mm new shares of common stock at 92.5% of net asset value per share, raising $70.5mm.

RIV pays out an appealing monthly distribution rate annualized at $1.53/share, yielding 13.38% on a forward basis. Distributions have held steady for the last few years following a rate cut at the beginning of 2023. A large proportion of the fund’s distributions have historically been a return of capital [ROC].

ROC is considered a non-dividend distribution. ROC has a tax-deferred benefit in which it will lower the cost basis of the investment until it reaches $0/share; from there, ROC will be taxed as ordinary income. This poses a dilemma for investors in determining which account RIV will be most suitable for; non-qualified dividends will be taxed at ordinary rates. I believe whether RIV is placed in a taxable or tax-advantaged account will be entirely up to the investor and whether they plan on using the income more immediately.

Though RIV provides an appealing multi-asset strategy, investors may consider peer strategies that may offer similar exposure and high-yielding benefits.

Comparing historical performance, the Special Opportunities Fund (SPE) and Cohen & Steers Closed-End Opportunities Fund (FOF) have each outperformed RIV during most holding periods on a total return basis.

In addition to this, investors may consider ETFs like the Saba Closed-End Funds ETF (CEFS) or the Calamos CEF Income & Arbitrage ETF (CCEF) as ETF alternatives to CEFs. For performance and fees, FOF may be one of the more appealing strategies as an alternative to RIV.

Risks Related to RIV

RIV is a multi-asset CEF designed to provide investors with growth & income. RIV’s investments in SPACs can be considered as speculative, as many companies that go public through this method may not be as high of quality as ones that enter the public markets through an initial public offering. CEFs trading at a discount present the risk of remaining at a discount and never trading at NAV.

Final Thoughts

RIV can provide investors with a broad range of assets through investment in CEFs, SPACs, BDCs, as well as other financial instruments that may generate income. In terms of making an investment decision, peer strategies like SPE or FOF may be more optimal investment opportunities given their historical track records and more moderate fees. Given these factors, I am recommending RIV with a Hold rating.

data.title: Analog Devices: An AI Infrastructure Enabler
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Analog Devices Inc. (ADI) is not your typical semiconductor company. It is actually one of my high-conviction pick as an AI infrastructure enabler. And this is why I am rating it A Buy because its high performance analog and power products sit inside the very AI systems, data centers, and automated factories that are now seeing the biggest capex growth. It is quietly powering the secular boom in AI, automation, and next-gen data centers. My investment thesis on ADI is quite straightforward: ADI's high performance analog, mixed signal and power solutions are quite critical to AI and ML systems, advanced robotics, Autonomous vehicles [AV] and cutting-edge industrial applications. And as these trends accelerate, ADI monetizes the surge in demand for precision signal processing and power management. All this is done while leveraging a resilient and a diversified business model. As CEO, Vincent Roche, said that

...Despite the geopolitical and macro turbulence, we remain undeterred and excited by the tremendous growth opportunities that we see over both the near and long terms....

And given that the company combined secular growth tailwinds with efficient cash generation and profitability, it gives me confidence that the company warrants a Buy rating.

Business Overview

Analog Devices Inc. is a leader in high-performance analog and mixed-processing, signal semiconductors. It has a strong franchise in power management and signal chain processing, which is basically the picks and shovels that bridge the physical and digital worlds. The company's chips convert real world analog signals such as sound, pressure, motion and power into digital data with extreme accuracy. ADI boasts 6 decades of domain expertise and a vast catalog of precision components. From sensors and data converters to RF, power ICs, and embedded processors. As I said earlier, ADI is a highly diversified business across end markets, and industrial and instrumentation is the largest segment because it takes about 50% of revenue. Then there is automotive, which does about 30% of revenues, and communication, which takes another 15%. And finally the last segment is taken by consumer electronics. It is important to mention that about 85% of ADI revenues come from Industrial, Auto and Comms and this provides some level of stability in terms of sales. I would say that the diversification in products has helped off, especially during the recent downturn. I also note that ADI enjoys a premium performance moat in its niche, as also highlighted by management:

...we tend to get a premium for our performance, and that's the innovation premium you've all heard me talk about, where performance matters...

This tends to make it hard for lower-end competitors to displace ADI. As a result, this combination of breadth and technical depth gives ADI a durable edge, especially as we now enter a new investment cycle in AI and automation.

Let's now look at some of the growth and profitability metrics for ADI.

Growth and Profitability

The company's latest results blew past expectations in Q3 2025. I say this because ADI had endured one of the longest down cycles in memory, as shown below. And Q3 2025 earnings report illustrated the sharp recovery. Revenues jumped to $2.88Bn, representing an increase of 25% y/y.

The industrial segment increased by 23% as factory automation and instrumentation bounced back strongly. Automotive also grew by 22% y/y. And lastly, Communications increased by over 40% due to AI related demand for cloud infrastructure. Even consumer electronics, which traditionally have been a weaker spot for ADI, increased by 21%. I note that ADI scored a C- growth grade as industrial sales started climbing each quarter since the trough last year. CFO Rich Puccio noted that industrial orders are growing across all the sub-sectors and geographies, and this is clear evidence that virtually all segments are in growth mode simultaneously.

I note that ADI maintained its excellent profitability through the cycle. I note that the company scored an excellent A+ profitability grade. And this is because the margins are now on an upswing. Last quarter's gross margin was 69.2%, and the operating margin stood at 42.2%. And both of them improved by 100 bps y/y in Q3 2025. Also management expects gross margins to recover to 70% as factory utilization normalizes and the rich industrial mix returns. Looking ahead, I believe ADI's operating margin will trend between 40% with volume growth, even accounting for higher R&D and normalizing incentive compensation.

The company is also a cash machine. This is because over the past 12 months, ADI generated $3.7Bn of FCFs. Yet management returned over $3.5Bn to shareholders in that period as dividends and buybacks. And this reiterates a policy of 100% FCFs return over the long term.

Now that we have gone through the growth and profitability of ADI, let's look at some of the growth drivers that I believe will drive ADI's stock price higher.

Growth Drivers

I believe ADI sits at the nexus of multiple secular growth trends. First and foremost, the AI data center boom is a an ongoing explosion in AI model training and cloud services that is driving massive capital investments in data centers. And ADI is quietly one of the winners behind the scenes. Yet every hyperscale server rack requires high speed optical connectivity and efficient power deliver. And this is exactly, what ADI provides. I have previously talked about optical connectivity in one of my articles titled "Marvell Technology Is Positioned Squarely At The Center Of The AI Trend" Please refer to get a better understanding on the same. Nevertheless, ADI's management has noted that there is "no letup" in AI spending patterns:

...Similarly, on the ATE side, there's no -- it appears to be no letup in the AI/data center CapEx spending patterns, which we also think helps us on the ATE side....

And this benefits ADI's broad portfolio. For example, ADI's optical transceivers and DSP chips enable the 800G+ connections between AI accelerator clusters, and its power management ICs maximize energy efficiency in server boards. These optical and power building blocks play a similar role to what digital networking vendors provide on the ASIC side, but ADI's revenue is spread across many OEMs and platforms instead of a few hyperscale design wins.

In Q3 2025, the comm segments accounted for 13% of revenue and grew about 4% sequentially and 37% y/y as AI driven cloud and networking demand continue to build. The company also scored 2 major design wins for next-gen cloud hardware. And this will begin generating revenue in 2026. These wins reinforce that ADI's content is growing at the cutting edge of AI infrastructure. And this simply means that as long as companies like Amazon (AMZN). Google (GOOG)(GOOGL), Microsoft (MSFT) and Meta (META) are pouring billions into AI compute, and then ADI will be selling more high-performance components into those data centers.

Secondly, there is factory automation and robotics. As of now there is a new wave of industrial automation, which is driven by labor shortages, according to McKinsey & Company. Remember, this is a core ADI territory, as I had mentioned earlier; therefore, I'd expect substantial growth from this, given that automation orders have increased in the last 2 orders. The real excitement is actually the future of robotics, which I believe ADI is well positioned for, given its core strength is in industry and automation.

Lastly, I'd say it's automotive electrification and ADAS. The auto industry's march towards electric vehicles and AV is another multiyear secular driver for ADI. Auto already represents 30% of ADI's revenue, and it has proven to be remarkably resilient.

Valuation

In regard to valuation, I note that the company scores a D valuation grade as shown below. Nevertheless, I note that the FWD Non-GAAP PEG stands at 1.56x while the sector median stands at 1.60x, implying that the stock is trading at a slight discount relative to the sector.

When I look at FWD Non-GAAP P/E, I note that ADI is trading at 29.91x while the sector median stands at 22.46x. For context, Texas Instruments (TXN) trades around 28x FWD Non-GAAP P/E, while NXP Semiconductors (NXPI) trades at about 16.24x, Microchip Technology (MCHP) at 35x, Marvel (MRVL) at 29x, and Broadcom (AVGO) about 50x. From an FWD Non-GAAP P/E perspective, I would say that ADI is trading at a slight discount to its peers with the exception of NXPI

Lastly in terms of the FWD EV/EBITDA multiple, I note that ADI stands at approximately 25x, while the sector median stands at 13x. Texas Instruments (TXN) for instance, is about 19x EBITDA, and NXP Semiconductors (NXPI) is at 12x. Marvel Technology (MRVL) is at 23x. ADI's multiple is a bit higher, reflecting its temporarily depressed EBITDA from the recent downturn and its superior margin profile. And this means that ADI is trading at a premium to peers by FWD EV/EBITDA. I expect this gap to narrow as ADI's EBITDA accelerates with the cyclical recovery.

Relative to Texas Instruments, ADI trades on a similar FWD P/E but leans a little more into high performance industrial, aerospace, and AI-driven communications, while TXN still skews more towards broad industrial and embedded. Versus NXPI, which is heavily tied to autos and trades at a lower multiple, I view ADI as the higher quality compounder with a broader end market mix and higher FCF margins. Compared to Marvell Technology, which is a pure digital networking and custom ASIC play for AI data centers, ADI's multiple is in the same ballpark, yet ADI delivers structurally higher gross margins and less earnings volatility because of its industrial and automotive base. And in my view, that balance of AI upside and defensive cash generation justifies ADI's current premium to the sector median and supports my Buy rating.

I would say that ADI's valuation is reasonable. And this is because with an approximate 20% EPS growth combined with a 70% gross margin on sale for 29X FWD Non-GAAP P/E. Given the company's technology leadership and improving outlook, I see this as a valuation disconnect that provides an attractive entry point. And hence the reason for assigning it a Buy rating.

ADI's Key Risks

The biggest risk I see for ADI as of now is macro downcycle risk. A global economic slowdown or another semiconductor inventory correction could hit ADI's near term sales and delay its recovery. There is also a risk that Geopolitical uncertainty could hurt ADI's revenues. For instance, export restrictions on China or tariff escalations could impact ADI's sizable international business. Given that industrial and automation forms a huge segment of ADI's revenues, and a huge portion of its supply chain comes from other countries such as China, then there is a definite risk that a disruption in the supply chain could really hurt ADI''s sales.

Final Thoughts

At the end of the day, ADI has positioned itself as a diversified player riding powerful secular trends in AI, automation, EV, and connectivity. ADI's role as an AI infrastructure enabler puts the company in a sweet spot to benefit from the capex upcycle of the coming years. And remember, I have demonstrated that all the diversified engines are all firing now. From factories to Cloud and EVs. And most importantly, management is executing well with financial discipline. And this is why I am giving ADI a Buy rating.

data.title: Politics And The Markets 11/27/25
data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

Seeking Alpha's Disclosure: Past performance is no guarantee of future results. No recommendation or advice is being given as to whether any investment is suitable for a particular investor. Any views or opinions expressed above may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker or US investment adviser or investment bank. Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.

data.title: PGIM Quant Solutions Large-Cap Core Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

U.S. equities notched record highs to end the quarter, primarily driven by easing monetary policy, strong corporate earnings, and artificial intelligence (AI) hype.

Russell 2000 small-cap stocks rallied substantially, experiencing their best quarter since 2021.

The U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) cut its target rate by 0.25% for the first time in nine months, prompted by a weakening labor market.

U.S. equities posted positive returns across the board for the quarter, with the Russell 2000 returning more than 12%, and the S&P 500 and S&P 400 indexes returning 8.12% and 5.55%, respectively.

Factor performance was mixed to start the quarter. However, value experienced a notable rebound in August across all market-cap segments, boosting relative returns. Growth as a style generally outperformed value stocks, while quality factors underperformed.

Quarter

After a volatile second quarter marked by unfolding global trade tensions, U.S. equities climbed to new record highs during the third quarter amid a calmer environment. The CBOE Volatility Index (VIX) returned to low-risk levels and remained well below the historical average. Investors were in “risk-on” mode, looking past the realities of continued stretched valuations and above-target inflation. The significant market rally during the quarter was widespread, with gains extending beyond mega-cap tech.

Easing global trade tensions contributed to the stable market landscape. The U.S. reached trade deal agreements with key trading partners such as Japan and the European Union in July, ahead of the month-end deadline. However, the Supreme Court will soon consider a challenge to reciprocal tariffs implemented by the current administration.

All eyes were on the Fed during the quarter as economic data sent mixed signals. Gross Domestic Product (GDP) grew 3.8% during the second quarter, following a 0.6% decline in the first quarter, offering conflicting signals about the health of the economy. The latest unemployment rate was 4.3% for August, which, although low by historical standards, has steadily increased since the beginning of the year. Coupled with a slowdown in job growth, signs point to a softening labor market. Inflation remains sticky, with recent readings above the Fed's 2% target. By mid-August, investors became undoubtedly optimistic that rate cuts were imminent after Fed Chair Powell's dovish comments. Ultimately, the Fed cut its target rate by 0.25% during its September meeting, its first cut in nine months. Current projections indicate two additional rate cuts this year.

The period was unique for small-cap equities. Following a bearish start to the year, small-cap stocks outperformed large-cap stocks, returning over 12% for the quarter. The outperformance was fueled by optimism surrounding easing monetary policy, as small-cap companies stand to reap the benefits of lower borrowing costs.

During the month of July, investors ventured into low-quality, speculative stocks, prompting a junk rally that proved to be short lived. Unprofitable companies in the small-cap space outperformed their positive earning counterparts by over 9% in September. This was most notable in the Information Technology sector, a phenomenon last observed in 2020.

Though the third quarter market rally was broad, mega-cap tech stocks remained the front-runners, driven by AI enthusiasm and strong earnings results. The Russell 1000 Growth Index returned over 10%, compared to around 5% for the Russell 1000 Value Index. The valuation disparity widened for the quarter, with the forward-looking Price-to-Earnings (P/E) multiple of the Russell 1000 Growth Index rising above 35, almost double that of the Value Index.

Factor performance was mixed for the period. Value and quality factors struggled, particularly in the small-cap space during July when low-quality, speculative stocks outperformed. However, August was a particularly strong month for our strategies. Value experienced a meaningful rebound, as investors rotated into undervalued parts of the market. Quality performance also improved markedly in tandem with value, boosting relative returns, before detracting again in September, most notably in the small-cap space. Growth factor performance was mixed to start the quarter, but outperformed value handily in September. Overall, the majority of our core strategies saw positive relative performance for the quarter. Nevertheless, uncertainty lies ahead, notably from global tensions and the pace of Fed easing.

Outlook and Positioning

PGIM Quantitative Solutions employs a quantitative, bottom-up investment process. Our proprietary stock selection model utilizes a carefully chosen suite of value, growth and quality factors with strong theoretical underpinnings to evaluate a broad universe of stocks. Our process is adaptive. Factors are dynamically weighted to reflect differences in individual stock growth rates. This ensures we are examining stocks through the right lenses.

Using the relative scores from the stock selection model, PGIM Quantitative Solutions constructs a Fund that efficiently targets factor exposures, while minimizing unintended risks and transactions costs. The result is a diversified core Fund that is positioned to produce consistent alpha over time.

Barron's: PGIM Investments ranked 4 out of 48, 8 out of 47, 12 out of 46 firms for the 1-, 5-, and 10-year periods ended 12/31/2024, respectively. See back page for methodology which takes into account Lipper rankings. PGIM Quant Solutions Large-Cap Core Fund (Class Z) Lipper total return ranking for the 1-, 3-, 5-, and 10-year periods as of 12/31/2024 for the Large-Cap Core Funds category were: 116/682, 72/620, 244/569, and 202/435, respectively. Lipper Funds category rankings are based on total return, do not take sales charges into account, and are calculated against all funds in each fund's respective Lipper category. Lipper total return ranking for the 1-, 3-, 5-, and 10-year periods as of 9/30/2025: 314/687, 227/642, 88/587 and 200/451, respectively. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

Average Annual Return as Of 9/30/2025

Total Returns (without sales charges) YTD 1-year 3-year 5-year 10-year Since 03/03/1999
PGIM Quant Solutions Large-Cap Core Equity Fund Z (PTEZX) 12.81 15.73 24.41 16.60 14.22 8.20
S&P 500 Index 14.83 17.60 24.94 16.47 15.30 8.52
SEC Standardized Returns (with sales charges)
PGIM Quant Solutions Large-Cap Core Equity Fund Z — 15.73 24.41 16.60 14.22 8.20

Source: PGIM Inc. Past performance does not guarantee future results. Current performance may be lower or higher than the past performance data quoted. Investment return and principal value will fluctuate so that an investor's shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than original cost. For the most recent month-end performance go to pgiminvestments.com .

Gross operating expense: Class Z, 0.56%. Net operating expense: Class Z, 0.45% after contractual reduction through 2/28/2026. Expenses are as of the most recent prospectus. Net operating expenses reflect expenses after fee waivers and/or expense reimbursements by PGIM Investments, if any. The contractual reduction date is the date through which PGIM Investments has agreed to waive fees or reimburse expenses, if applicable. Expenses for the current year may exceed the net operating expenses listed above due to exclusions from any applicable contractual waiver or reimbursement, which may fluctuate. PGIM Investments may recoup certain waived fees or reimbursed expenses. See the prospectus for more information.

Class Z shares may be available to group retirement plans and institutional investors through certain retirement, mutual fund wrap and asset allocation programs. They may also be available to institutional investors and through fee- or commission-based retail brokerage programs of certain financial intermediaries. Class Z shares are generally closed to new retirement plans. Please see the prospectus for additional information about fees, expenses, and investor eligibility.

Annualized returns without sales charges describe the return to the investor before any sales charges are imposed. SEC standardized return describes the return to the investor after maximum sales charges are imposed. All returns assume share price changes, as well as the compounding effect of reinvested dividends and capital gains. Returns may reflect fee waivers and/or expense reimbursements. Without such, returns would be lower. Performance by share class may vary. The performance data featured represents past performance for a period of less than one year. While past performance is never an indication of future results, short periods of performance may be particularly unrepresentative of long-term performance for certain types of funds.

After-tax returns are calculated using the highest historical individual federal marginal income tax rates and exclude state and local taxes. Actual after-tax returns depend on an investor's tax situation. Returns shown do not apply to shares held in tax-deferred arrangements (i.e., 401(k), IRAs). The Fund may invest in foreign securities , which are subject to currency fluctuation and political uncertainty; and derivative securities , which may carry market, credit, and liquidity risks. The Fund may use hedging techniques to help achieve its objective. These risks may result in greater share price volatility. There is no guarantee the Fund's objective will be achieved. Holdings are subject to change. An investment cannot be made directly into an index.

Risk Information —Risks of investing in the fund include but are not limited to the following: Large-capitalization companies go in and out of favor based on market and economic conditions; the Fund's value may not rise or fall as much as the value of funds that emphasize companies with smaller market capitalizations. The Fund's blend investment style may subject the Fund to the risks of both value and growth investing. Equity and equity-related securities may be subject to changes in value, and their values may be more volatile than those of other asset classes. The Fund is subject to the risk that the design of the subadviser's quantitative models may be flawed or incomplete, and the risk of error in the implementation of the models. There is no guarantee the Fund's objective will be achieved. Risks are more fully explained in the fund's prospectus.

The S&P 500 Index is a product of S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC and/or its affiliates and has been licensed for use by PGIM Quantitative Solutions LLC. Copyright © 2025 S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC, a division of S&P Global, Inc. (SPGI), and/or its affiliates. All rights reserved. Redistribution or reproduction in whole or in part are prohibited without written permission of S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC. For more information on any of S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC's indices please visit www.spdji.com . S&P is a registered trademark of S&P Global and Dow Jones is a registered trademark of Dow Jones Trademark Holdings LLC.

Standard & Poor's 500 (S&P 500) Index is an unmanaged market capitalization-weighted index of 500 stocks of large U.S. companies. Russell 1000 Index is an unmanaged index that measures the performance of the large-cap segment of the U.S. equity universe. It is a subset of the Russell 3000 Index and includes approximately 1,000 of the largest securities based on a combination of their market cap and current index membership. The Russell 1000 represents approximately 92% of the U.S. market. Russell 2000 Index measures the performance of the small-cap segment of the U.S. equity universe. S&P 400 Index provides investors with a benchmark for mid-sized companies.

The comments, opinions and estimates contained herein are based on, or derived from, publicly available information from sources that PGIM Quantitative Solutions believes to be reliable. We do not guarantee their accuracy. The material is for informational purposes only and sets forth our views as of this date. The underlying assumptions and these views are subject to change. Past performance is not a guarantee of future results. The material may not be reproduced or distributed to any other person or entity.

Barron's: Used with permission. ©2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc. Source: Barron's, Feb. 27, 2025. Barron's rankings are based on asset-weighted returns in funds in five categories: U.S. Equity; World Equity; Mixed Asset; Taxable Bond; and Tax-Exempt (each a "Barron's ranking category"). Rankings also take into account an individual fund's performance within its Lipper peer universe. Lipper calculated each fund's net total return for the year ended Dec. 31, 2024, minus the effects of 12b-1 fees and sales charges. Each fund in the survey was given a percentile ranking, with 100 the highest and 1 the lowest in its category. That ranking measured how a fund compared with its peer "universe," as tracked by Lipper, not just the funds in the survey. Individual fund scores were then multiplied by the 2024 weighting of their Barron's ranking category as determined by the entire Lipper universe of funds. Those fund scores were then totaled, creating an overall score and ranking for each fund family in the survey in each Barron's ranking category. To qualify for the ranking, firms must offer at least three active mutual funds or actively run ETFs in Lipper's general U.S. Stock category; one in World Equity; and one Mixed Asset. They also need to offer at least two taxable bond funds and one national tax-exempt bond fund. All funds must have a track record of at least one year.
