data.title: Vale: A Long-Term Buy Despite Near-Term Noise
data.description:
Introduction

Last time I covered Vale (VALE), I highlighted their strong margins and significant long-term growth potential in energy transition metals, with strong fundamentals and ramp-ups that can support their long-term potential.

With a good report released recently, solid fundamentals, continued investments, and the same strong long-term tailwinds outweighing the near-term headwinds and potential for more lawsuit issues, Vale remains a Buy for me.

Internal Developments

As we can see, Vale reported a very solid FCF in Q3 (~$2.57 billion), reaching a FCF of $4.08 billion over the first 9 months of 2025. Still, Vale is expecting Q4 to come with higher CAPEX, expecting $5.4 billion to $5.7 billion for the whole year and spending only $3.78 billion in 9M'25, so there's $1.62 billion to $1.82 billion left for Q4.

Financially, based on Vale's latest report, we see a solid position, with the current assets covering the current liabilities and not that much debt. Still, we see the debt increasing quite a bit since Vale now has a higher net debt target, allowing them to invest and expand at better rates while taking advantage of the current environment.

A very important risk to keep in mind that the market may be overlooking now is that BHP (BHP) - analyzed recently and rated a Buy - recently lost a new legal battle in the UK related to the 2015 Brazil dam collapse (i.e. Brazil's worst environmental disaster, which BHP and Vale also lost in Brazil a few years ago), in a lawsuit brought by over 600,000 Brazilian people, businesses, and local governments, with the company making a new $500 million worth of provisions following this. You may be inclined to think that this is only for BHP, but BHP had a "contribution claim" against Vale for this case as well that was later replaced by an agreement in July 2024, with both of them agreeing to pay 50-50 for anything related to the UK/Dutch group actions.

Still, since they already settled this in Brazil and paid (and will continue to pay) compensation based on an approved long-term plan, BHP expects this to "significantly reduce the size and value of claims in the U.K. group action," with the new ruling citing that "BHP are strictly liable as polluters in respect of damage caused by the collapse" under environmental law, not under corporate law - potentially exposing them to even more costs (which they'll appeal), so this issue is not yet over.

Macro Developments

Something important to keep in mind is that Brazil's interest rates were maintained around the same levels, some of the highest since the early 2000s. The country's Central Bank expects to maintain the SELIC rate at around 15% and enter into a "very prolonged pause" at this level, but we should keep in mind that when they are ready to cut them and continue the long-term trend of lower and lower rates, this can boost Vale in several ways.

First of all, this would lower their cost of debt, which is already generally low since Brazil's companies get some preferred rates from BNDES (Banco Nacional de Desenvolvimento Econômico e Social), which can be excellent, especially now since they've increased their net debt limits. Rate cuts can also boost the economy and incentivize investments thanks to it and supporting the country's demand for Vale's iron.

Plus, this would also lower the risk-free rate and lead to more interest for Brazil's high-yield stocks, especially with global rate cuts on top changing the risk-adjusted yields globally. Again, since Brazil has a mandatory minimum dividend of 25% of a company's profit and they generally have excellent profits, margins, and costs, the dividend will always be very solid, especially if the valuations remain low, so it's not hard to see how these yields can be attractive for the global markets once global rate cuts advance (see the US for example, where treasuries are still at a solid ~4% now).

But, something I noted in the Petrobras (PBR) analysis posted recently is that we've seen an important change coming from the Brazilian Senate approving the reinstatement of a 10% withholding tax on dividends paid to nonresident shareholders, which is part of Brazil's wider tax reform initiatives. The exact implications ultimately depend on your country of residency, so make sure you double-check for your particular situation. I don't really mind it, and I'm used to even bigger ones, but it's of course hurting the yield, so it can have an impact on the market's perceived value once it gets enforced.

Another important factor to take into account is the recent Trump-Xi deal, where the US and China would reportedly work towards easing the previously-imposed chip export ban, reopen the market for US farmers (which China had previously halted for many months, hurting US farmers), and more, potentially being good for the international markets since these two are key for global trade.

These are on top of the rollback on some tariffs, including Brazilian coffee, fruit, beef, and more, potentially leading the way for more improvements, although it's worth mentioning that Vale doesn't import that much to the US, but the country still has an impact on global prices.

On top of China's policy shifts that should be good for the long term, Vale recently saw potential from India, with rising demand from the country helping to offset the stagnant demand we've seen from China, with Vale's CEO expecting India's steel production capacity to even double in the following 5 to 7 years, albeit China will remain the top consumer and producer in these industries for ages, accounting for ~60% of Vale's sales.

Most things are advancing in an overall good direction for Vale since the previous analysis, but there are still several risks to remember in this case, especially if we think about the geopolitical environment. Trump's relationship with Brazil and BRICS remains quite tense, and the country ultimately plays a very big role in the global commodity market, which makes it a very volatile environment to be in.

But, similar to Petrobras, Vale has been going through this environment (and even worse) for many decades, and I'm glad to see that they're ramping up their investments and increasing the net debt target, taking advantage of more than just iron going forward.

Updated Valuation

Based on Vale's 9M results and CAPEX expectations, I'll start the valuation with $4.25 billion in FCF in 2025, expecting a 4.5% CAGR for the first 5 years thanks to their ramped-up investments and copper's and nickel's potential, as well as the prospects of China's demand recovering in the future, followed by a 3.5% CAGR for the subsequent 5 thanks to their future-facing commodities, with a 2.5% terminal growth rate. Once again, I'll assume a $1.5 billion jump from 2027 onwards thanks to potentially better CAPEX and projects/production ramping up together with improved costs. I'll go for a WACC of 10.5% (above my estimated 9.88% to be a bit more conservative; assumptions: ~11.2% cost of equity, ~6.5% cost of debt), with $16.64 billion in expanded net debt (takes into account dam failure provisions), along with 4.27 billion shares outstanding based on the Q3 report. This way, we get to an enterprise value of $75.70 billion, an equity value of $59.06 billion, and an intrinsic value of $13.83 per share.

Again, I believe $4.25 billion as a base remains very low (even with the $1.5 billion injection) to assume as an average in this case, but it offers a good indicator of a relatively fair value below which the margin of safety would be great, with significantly higher potential in good times.

Conclusions

Vale remains a Buy in my opinion, with solid fundamentals, ramped-up investments, and a key role in our future thanks to their vital commodities, likely continuing to pay a very solid dividend over the long term. Risks remain, especially given the near-term headwinds and political uncertainty, but Vale is definitely a solid option to consider for a long-term-oriented portfolio.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
